---
title: "Has Anything Actually Changed? Post-Apartheid Labor Outcomes in South Africa"
subtitle: "DSAN 5650: Causal Inference for Computational Social Science"
authors:
  - name: Erin Brzusek
    affiliation: Georgetown University<br>Data Science and Analytics
    email: eb1496@georgetown.edu
date: 2026-08-05
bibliography: library_zortero.bib
csl: chicago-17-no-url.csl
title-block-banner: "#041e42"
title-block-banner-color: "#e3e3e5"
abstract: |
  This study seeks to examine the long-term economic effects of legalized discrimination in post-Apartheid South Africa. Recreating the methodological framework of previous research on the effects of government-mandated racial classification, I utilize data from the 2001 and 2011 South African Census to evaluate labor outcomes. Specifically, I analyze income disparities among men and women across "White" and "Coloured" racial categories. Utilizing Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression models, I analyze income and assess the extent to which historical socioeconomic divides persist. By recreating the previous analysis with more recent census data, this project provides insights into the current state of economic inequality in South Africa.
---

## Introduction

The history of South Africa stands as a fascinating case study for the field of `Causal Inference for Computational Social Science`. The Apartheid era was black-and-white legalized discrimination, *literally*. The implementation of the National Party's ideological framework hinged on the Population Registration Act of 1950, which required every citizen to be classified and registered under racial categories. The government was then able to dictate an individual's rights from "cradle to grave" [@lawal_april_2024]. Subsequent legislation such as the Group Areas Act forced non-white populations into the country’s most economically unproductive areas, while "pass laws" criminalized the unregulated movement of Coloured citizens within urban spaces. Systemic oppression extended to all individual pursuits: schools were segregated and interpersonal relationships across racial lines were legally barred through measures like the Prohibition of Mixed Marriages Act. 

In their foundational study examining the direct effects of these Apartheid-era classifications, researchers utilized historical data to demonstrate that being classified as White yielded a massive 4.5x increase in income for men and an average of 5 additional years of formal education. These advantages explained approximately 94% of the total observed mean income disparity between White and Coloured men [@pellicer_understanding_2023]. 

The contact hypothesis [@bornman_patterns_2011] suggests a lack of contact between groups leads to negative attitudes. To foster improved intergroup attitudes, researchers argue that specific conditions must be met: equal status, shared goals, and the development of personal relationships supported and condoned by authorities. Whether post-Apartheid legislative reforms have effectively cultivated these conditions remains an open question. While all South Africans are now constitutionally recognized as equals, the state has implemented aggressive interventions, such as equity quotas for businesses with more than 50 employees, to actively redress historical disenfranchisement [@sakeliga_quotas_2025]. These policies remain highly contentious; critics characterize them as unconstitutional forms of social engineering. These competing sociological and political realities underscore a fundamental economic inquiry: *in the wake of Apartheid, to what extent have labor market disparities between racial categories actually changed?*

This study aims to tackle that question by executing a pooled cross-sectional replication of the original research framework. Utilizing a 10% sample of microdata from the 2001 and 2011 South African Censuses, I analyze a cohort following the formal end of Apartheid in 1994. By stratifying the sample by gender and executing Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression models that control for age, employment status, and education level, I directly estimate income. Ultimately, this paper evaluates whether the racial wage gap between "White" and "Coloured" cohorts has shrunk, stagnated, or increased, providing fresh insights into South African labor outcomes.

## Data

According to their website: "Censuses are principal means of collecting basic population and housing statistics required for social and economic development, policy interventions, their implementation and evaluation. The Post-Apartheid South African government has conducted three Censuses, in 1996, 2001 and 2011."

For the purposes of this study, the 2001 and 2011 datasets were utilized. Both datasets are a 10% sample. 

[@statssa_census_2011]

Did not keep unspecified income (339531 ~7.8%) or unknown employment status (3222 ~0.1%) in data, removed entries 

[@statssa_census_2001]

## Exploratory Analysis

**Tables**

Here is the 2001 summary table

![2001 summary table](images/formatted_table_2001.png){#fig-2001-table}

Here is the 2011 summary table

![2011 summary table](images/formatted_table_2011.png){#fig-2001-table}

**Income Graphs by Gender**

Here's the 2001 census data

![2001 income density plot by gender](images/2001_income.png){#fig-2001-income}

Here is the 2011 census data

![2011 income density plot by gender](images/2011_income.png){#fig-2011-income}

## Models

Pushing Torwards the Asymptote of Causality. . . 

The original study had to use a fuzzy regression discontinuity approach. 

The data was separated for men and women and aggregated by census year to mirror the original study.

### Ordinary Least Squares on 2011 Alone

Most Basic Model (2011 only)

![2011 OLS results](images/stargazer_2011_ols.png){#fig-2011-OLS}

### OLS on Pooled Data

Pooling (2001 & 2011)

A prioi threat to identification: income has a strong non-linear life-cycle profile. Introducing 2001 data should help to account for non-linearities due to life cycle patterns. 

![2011 OLS results](images/stargazer_pooled_ols.png){#fig-pooled-OLS}

## Evaluation

Evaluation Section

![Wage Premium](images/wage_premium_shift.png){#fig-wage-bar-chart}

## Conclusion

Conclude

## Acknowledgments {.unnumbered}

Professor Jacobs

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
